questions.question: Explain the difference between zombie and orphan processes in UNIX/Linux.
keyPoints: Zombie: Child terminated but parent hasn't collected exit status using wait()
Orphan: Child whose parent has terminated - adopted by init process
Zombies remain in process table; orphans continue running with new parent

questions.question: Describe the purpose of WNOHANG and WUNTRACED options in waitpid().
keyPoints: WNOHANG: Makes waitpid() return immediately if no child has exited (non-blocking)
WUNTRACED: Reports status of stopped processes in addition to terminated ones
Used for polling and job control respectively

questions.question: Write a C code snippet showing how to use waitpid() to wait for a specific child process.
keyPoints: Include headers: sys/wait.h, unistd.h
Use waitpid(pid, &status, 0) where pid is child PID
Check return value for success (-1 for error, 0 for WNOHANG, pid for success)
Use WIFEXITED and WEXITSTATUS macros to get exit code

questions.question: Explain the purpose of wait3() and wait4() functions compared to wait() and waitpid().
keyPoints: wait3() provides resource usage (struct rusage) of child
wait4() combines waitpid() functionality with resource usage
Allows parent to monitor CPU time used by child
Useful for accounting and profiling

questions.question: List and explain at least four macros used to interpret the status returned by wait() functions.
keyPoints: WIFEXITED(status): True if normal termination
WEXITSTATUS(status): Exit code if normal termination
WIFSIGNALED(status): True if killed by signal
WTERMSIG(status): Signal number that killed process
WIFSTOPPED(status): True if stopped by signal

questions.question: Write a complete C program that creates a child process, and the parent uses waitpid() to wait for the child with proper status checking. Include handling for both normal termination and signal termination.
keyPoints: Fork child process
In child: either exit() with status or get killed by signal
In parent: call waitpid() with WNOHANG for non-blocking check
Use WIFEXITED/WEXITSTATUS for normal exit
Use WIFSIGNALED/WTERMSIG for signal death
Print appropriate messages
Handle errors appropriately

questions.question: Explain the problem of zombie processes in UNIX. How can a parent process prevent its children from becoming zombies? Discuss multiple approaches.
keyPoints: Zombie created when child terminates but parent doesn't call wait()
Zombies occupy process table entries
Approach 1: Call wait()/waitpid() after fork()
Approach 2: Use waitpid() with WNOHANG in a loop
Approach 3: Install SIGCHLD handler with wait() inside
Approach 4: Double fork technique - child forks another child that becomes orphan
Each approach explained with trade-offs

questions.question: Compare wait(), waitpid(), wait3(), and wait4() functions. Discuss their differences, parameters, and use cases.
keyPoints: wait(): Basic - waits for any child, no resource info
waitpid(): Waits for specific pid, options available
wait3(): Like wait() but returns rusage info
wait4(): Combines waitpid() and wait3() - specific pid + rusage
Parameter comparison: pid, status, options, rusage
Use cases: Simple sync, specific child monitoring, resource accounting

questions.question: Design a program that demonstrates process monitoring where a parent creates multiple children and waits for them using waitpid() with WNOHANG option to avoid blocking.
keyPoints: Create multiple child processes using loop
Each child does some work and exits with different status
Parent continues other work while checking children
Use waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) in a loop
Collect all children until no more to collect
Report each child's exit status appropriately
Handle edge cases properly
